1.3 Matrices

One of the motivations to have matrices is when transforming coordinate systems.

which can be written as

Definition 1.3.1.

Let be an matrix and be an matrix.

The matrix product is the matrix whose -entry is

Equivalently, the -entry of is obtained by taking the dot product of the th row of with the th column of .

The product is defined only when the number of columns of equals the number of rows of .

Definition 1.3.2.

For a matrix

its determinant is defined by

Definition 1.3.3.

For a matrix

its determinant is defined by

Definition 1.3.4.

Let be a square matrix. The -minor of , denoted , is the determinant of the matrix obtained from by deleting the th row and the th column.

Definition 1.3.5.

Let be a square matrix. The -cofactor of , denoted , is defined by

Definition 1.3.6.

Let be an matrix. The transpose of , denoted , is the matrix whose -entry is

The determinant measures how a square matrix scales signed area in two dimensions and signed volume in three dimensions.

Matrix algebra satisfies many properties for a nice algebraic structure.

  1. Associative

    Theorem 1.3.7.

    In other words, if is a vector and and are matrices,

    This theorem could be interpreted with the lens of matrices as transformations. The transformation is equivalent to applying the transformation described by first, then transformation described by matrix .

  2. Commutative However, note that , which makes sense after the above theorem. In fact, given , is not even necessarily well-defined.

  3. Multiplicative Identity There exist an unique identity fixing the height of the matrix being multiplied, with along the diagonal and elsewhere. Denote it as , we have

    1. Multiplicative Inverse

      Definition 1.3.8.

      For a matrix , matrix is its inverse iff , where is the identity matrix.

      Observe that a matrix has an inverse only if it is a square matrix, meaning it has the same number of rows and columns. In fact, any square matrix with a non-zero determinant has an inverse. Furthermore, finding the solution to a system of linear equations is equivalent to finding the inverse of a matrix. Consider a system of linear equations regarding variables. If is an n-by-n square matrix representing the coefficients of the variables in each equation, a vector representing the solutions, and representing the vector of the constants on the right side of the equations, then we have

      Thus, how to find the inverse of a matrix becomes a natural question. Here, I claim, without proof, that if the adjunct matrix of a square matrix is obtained by taking the transpose of the co-factor of the minors of , then

Example 1.3.9.

Consider the matrix

Then

so rotates every vector in the plane by counterclockwise about the origin.

In particular,

and

Thus, a system described by and is mapped to a system of and . Therefore, counterclockwise.
